1: hoste, adelphoi mou agapetoi kai epipotheto, chara kai stephanos mou: 'therefore' (hoste) draws the conclusion of 3:17-21. Agapetoi kai epipotheto (beloved and longed for): the doubled emotional language is unusually warm. Stephanos (crown): the victor's wreath of the athletic games, not the royal diadem (diadem). Paul uses the same crown-image in 1 Thess 2:19-20 where the community itself is his crown at Christ's Parousia. The crown is eschatological — not present status but anticipated reward. Stekete en Kyrio: stand firm in the Lord — the imperative that 3:20-21's Parousia hope grounds.
2: Euodian parakaleo kai Syntichen parakaleo to auto phronein en Kyrio: the only named individuals addressed in the main body of the letter (apart from Epaphroditus and Timothy). Both names are Greek — Euodia (Good Journey) and Syntyche (Happy Chance). The identical formula applied to each (parakaleo...) gives neither precedence. The dispute between them is not specified. To auto phronein (be of the same mind): the phronein root is the letter's key term — phronein en Kyrio means having the same Christ-oriented mindset that Paul described in 2:5 (let this mind be in you which was in Christ Jesus).
3: nai eroto kai se, gnesie syzyge: 'true/genuine companion' (gnesie syzyge). Gnesios (genuine, dispute level 2): MKT 'true companion'; lit. legitimately born, genuine — the opposite of counterfeit. The identity of this 'true companion' is debated: Epaphroditus, Luke, Silas, or even Paul's wife (though the masculine adjective makes the last unlikely). Biblo tes zoes (book of life, dispute level 2 — zoe): the eschatological register of the righteous (Exod 32:32-33; Isa 4:3; Dan 12:1; Rev 3:5; 13:8; 17:8; 20:12-15).
4: chairete en Kyrio pantote: the double imperative rejoice — the repetition is emphatic. Chairō (rejoice/be glad): MKT 'rejoice'; the word family includes 'grace' (charis) and 'gift' (charisma) — all derived from the root of gladness/favor. The qualifier en Kyrio (in the Lord): rejoicing is not a psychological technique but a location — located within the sphere of Christ's lordship, which guarantees the Parousia-hope (3:20-21) and therefore grounds present joy regardless of circumstances (Paul writes from prison). Pantote (always): no circumstantial exception.
5: to epieikes hymon gnostheto pasin anthropois: let your epieikes be known to all people. Epieikes (gentleness, forbearance, reasonableness, moderation): one of the most difficult Greek virtue-terms to translate. It occurs in 1 Tim 3:3; Tit 3:2; James 3:17; 1 Pet 2:18. Aristotle defined it as equity that corrects strict legal justice when strict application would be unjust (Nicomachean Ethics 5.10). In the LXX it renders the Hebrew concept of patient, non-retaliatory response. MKT 'gentleness' captures one dimension. Ho Kyrios eggys: the Lord is near (eggys — close, proximate). Ambiguous: spatial (the Lord is close — omnipresent) or temporal (the Parousia is imminent, cf. 3:20-21; 1 Cor 16:22; Rev 22:20). The temporal reading connects to the entire eschatological logic of ch 3-4.
6: meden merimnate: be anxious about nothing (merimnaō — to be distracted, divided in one's concerns, pulled in different directions). The same verb family appears in Matt 6:25-34 (do not worry) and Luke 10:41 (Martha is worried/distracted about many things). En panti...: in every situation, by prayer (proseuche — general prayer), petition (deesis — specific request), and thanksgiving (eucharistia) — three modes of address to God. The thanksgiving embedded in request is the Philippians' characteristic posture (1:3). To aitema hymon (your requests): the content of the petitions presented before God.
7: he eirene tou theou he hyperechousa panta noun phrouresei tas kardias hymon kai ta noemata hymon en Christo Iesou: the peace of God surpassing all understanding will guard your hearts and minds. Eirene (peace): the Greek rendering of Hebrew shalom — comprehensive well-being, not merely absence of conflict. Hyperechousa panta noun: surpassing (hyperecho — to excel, be superior to) all mind/understanding. Phroureō (guard): military verb — to stand sentinel, post a guard; used for soldiers guarding a city gate or a prisoner. The peace of God is personified as a military guard standing watch over the hearts and minds. En Christo Iesou: in Christ Jesus — the sphere within which the guarding operates.
8: hosa estin alethē, hosa semna, hosa dikaia, hosa hagna, hosa prosphile, hosa euphema: the virtue catalogue of six hosa (whatever) clauses. Semna (noble/honorable/worthy of respect): the adjective for what commands reverence — used in 1 Tim 3:8, 11; Tit 2:2. Arete (virtue, excellence): the only occurrence of this key Stoic philosophical term in Paul. Arete is the central concept of Greek ethics — the excellence proper to a thing or person (Aristotle); the four cardinal virtues of Stoicism. Paul uses it without apology, demonstrating that Greek ethical vocabulary can serve Christian formation. Epainos (praise, commendation): what merits approval. The list moves from ontological qualities (true, noble, right, pure) to aesthetic ones (lovely, admirable) to the encompassing virtue-term and the functional criterion (praise).
9: ha kai emathete kai parelabete kai ekousate kai eidete en emoi: four verbs of reception — learned, received, heard, seen. Parelabete (received): the technical term for tradition-transmission (1 Cor 11:23; 15:3) — the Philippians received the apostolic tradition from Paul. Ekousate (heard): auditory reception of Paul's teaching. Eidete en emoi (seen in me): the apostle himself is the embodied example of the teaching — his conduct is the lived tradition the community observed. Tauta prassete: practice these things. Ho de theos tes eirenes estai meth' hymon: the God of peace (cf. the peace of God, v.7) will be with them — the divine presence as the consequence of Christ-shaped practice.
10: Echarēn de en Kyrio megalos: I rejoiced greatly in the Lord — the joy-theme (v.4) applied to Paul's own experience. Hoti ede pote anethaleite to hyper emou phronein: that your thinking-for-me has revived (anathallo — to bloom again, revive; used of plants putting out new growth). Phronein hyper emou (thinking/caring for me): the phronein root again — the letter's key term now applied to the community's practical concern for Paul. Ef' ho kai ephroneit' akaireisthe de: indeed you were thinking of me — you lacked the opportunity. Paul preemptively softens any implication of neglect: they were concerned but circumstantially unable to help.
11: ouch hoti ephzeto to hysterema lego: not that I speak from need (hysterema — what is lacking, the deficit). Ego gar emathon en hois eimi autarkes einai: I have learned (emathon — aorist, completed learning) to be content (autarkes) in whatever circumstances. Autarkes (self-sufficient, content): the Stoic term for self-sufficiency — the wise person's independence from external circumstances. The Stoic autarkeia was achieved through the development of inner rational virtue. Paul uses the vocabulary but fundamentally reorients it: he has learned contentment, not achieved it through native virtue; and the sufficiency comes from Christ who strengthens him (v.13), not from inner resources.
12: oida kai tapeinousthai, oida kai perisseuein: I know how to be humbled (tapeinoomai — the verb of Christ's kenosis in 2:8) and how to abound. En panti kai en pasin memyemai: in all things and in all circumstances I have been initiated (myeō — perfect passive: to initiate into a mystery; to instruct in the secrets). Myeō is the technical vocabulary of Hellenistic mystery religions: to be initiated into the sacred rites. Paul adopts the vocabulary while inverting the source: he was 'initiated' not by a mystery cult but by Christ — the 'secret' (mysterion) of contentment through Christ's empowering. The verb appears only here in the NT.
13: panta ischyo en to endynamounti me Christo: I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me. Ischyo (to be strong, able, avail): general strength-capability. Endynamoō (strengthen): the en-prefix intensifying dynamoō (to empower from dynamis — power). The verse is directly connected to the contentment claim of vv.11-12: 'all things' refers to all the circumstances of abasement and abundance just listed, not an unlimited universal capability. The strengthening comes from Christ himself — the one who 'works in you to will and to act' (2:13: ho theos estin ho energōn en hymin).
14: plen kalos epoiesate synkoinonesantes mou te thlipsei: yet it was well done of you to share (synkoinoneo — to participate together with, to co-fellowship) in my affliction (thlipsis). The syn-prefix in synkoinoneo recurs throughout Philippians — the sharing and participation that characterizes authentic community (1:7: synkoinonous mou tes charitos; 1:5: koinonia eis to euangelion). Even as Paul distances himself from material need (vv.11-12), he values the Philippians' participation itself as a relational act of solidarity.
15: oidate de kai hymeis, Philippesioi, hoti en arche tou euangeliou: the Philippians know their own history with Paul. Arche tou euangeliou (the beginning of the gospel): Paul's first missionary campaign in Europe, ~49-50 CE. Logon doseos kai lempseos: the accounting of giving and receiving (logos in the commercial/bookkeeping sense — the account balance). Logos (dispute level 2): here clearly the financial-accounting sense (credit/debit ledger), not the theological Word. Only the Philippian church had a giving-and-receiving relationship with Paul — they were his unique financial partners in mission.
16: hoti kai en Thessalonike kai hapax kai dis eis ten chreian moi epempsate: even when I was in Thessalonica, you sent to my need (chreia) more than once (hapax kai dis — once and twice = repeatedly). This is confirmed by 1 Thess 2:9 (Paul worked with his hands in Thessalonica) and Acts 17:1-9 (brief Thessalonian ministry). The Philippian support supplemented Paul's manual labor income during the Thessalonian mission. The historicity of the detail grounds the gratitude in specific remembered acts of generosity.
17: ouch hoti epizeteo to doma, alla epizeteo ton karpont on pleonazonta eis logon hymon: not that I seek the gift (doma — the concrete transfer of goods), but I seek the fruit (karpos) that increases to your account (logos — the ledger entry). Logos (dispute level 2): again the commercial-accounting sense. The eschatological reversal: Paul does not want his financial account credited but theirs. Their generosity will accumulate in the divine ledger as credit toward the eschatological judgment (cf. 2 Cor 9:6-11; Matt 6:20: store up treasures in heaven).
18: apecho de panta kai perisseuō: I have received full payment (apecho — the technical commercial receipt verb, used on papyri receipts meaning 'paid in full'; cf. Matt 6:2, 5, 16 where the hypocrites 'have received their reward' — apechousin). Paul issues a formal receipt. Osme euodias, thysian dekten, euareston to theo: a pleasing aroma (euodia), an acceptable sacrifice (thysia dekte), well-pleasing to God (euarestos). The sacrificial-cultic vocabulary comes from the LXX: osme euodias appears in Gen 8:21 (Noah's offering) and repeatedly in Exod-Lev for acceptable burnt offerings. The Philippians' financial gift is reframed as a priestly offering ascending to God.
19: ho de theos mou plerose pasan chreian hymon kata to ploutos autou en doxe en Christo Iesou: my God will supply (pleroō — fill full, satisfy completely) all your need (chreian — the same word used of Paul's need in v.16) according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus. The logical reciprocity: they supplied Paul's need; God will supply theirs. Kata to ploutos autou en doxe: according to his wealth/riches in glory — not a miserly minimum but a supply proportioned to divine abundance. En Christo Iesou: the Christ-sphere is the medium through which God's supply comes.
20: to de theo kai patri hemon he doxa eis tous aionas ton aionon. amen: the doxology. To theo kai patri hemon: to our God and Father — the letter's characteristic double designation (1:2: God our Father). He doxa (the glory): doxa (dispute in wider vocabulary; here unambiguous doxological use). Eis tous aionas ton aionon (unto the ages of the ages): the Hebrew idiom of temporal infinity — the Amen-formula closes the letter's body before the final greetings.
21: aspasasthe panta hagion en Christo Iesou: greet every saint in Christ Jesus. Hagion (holy one, saint — hagios, dispute level 2): those set apart for God. Hoi syn emoi adelphoi: the brothers with Paul — his co-workers in the place of imprisonment, probably Rome.
22: aspazontai hymas pantes hoi hagioi, malista de hoi ek tes Kaisaros oikias: all the saints greet you, especially those from Caesar's household (Kaisaros oikia). Kaisaros oikia is not the imperial family but the vast network of slaves and freedmen who administered Roman imperial operations — found in cities throughout the empire. Inscriptional evidence (CIL VI) documents hundreds of imperial slaves and freedmen at Rome. Their greetings from Rome to Philippi — a Roman colony — represent the gospel's penetration into the imperial administrative apparatus, a remarkable irony given that Paul writes from Roman custody.
23: he charis tou Kyriou Iesou Christou meta tou pneumatos hymon: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit (pneuma). The singular pneumatos hymon (your spirit — singular, not plural 'spirits') treats the community as a single collective person, or uses spirit for the inner life of each member. The closing grace-benediction is Pauline standard (Gal 6:18; Phlm 25; 1 Cor 16:23; 2 Cor 13:14); here the spirit/grace pairing connects to the Spirit-Christ relationship throughout the letter. Amen: the liturgical assent that closes the letter in public worship.
